title: Apply: wave properties
description: Apply your understanding of wave properties in this set of free, standards-aligned practice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
Snapshots of four periodic waves are modeled below. Each wave is modeled on the same grid.

**Rank the waves from longest wavelength to shortest wavelength.**

*longest wavelength*

[[☃ sorter 1]]

*shortest wavelength*
hints: **Wavelength** is the distance from the beginning of one wave cycle to the beginning of the next cycle.

Wavelength can be measured from a crest to the next crest, or from a trough to the next trough. So, to compare the wavelengths we can count the number of squares from a peak to the next peak, or from a trough to the next trough.

*Note that wavelength is separate from amplitude. On the models above, amplitude can be measured as how far the wave extends above and below the equilibrium line.*

From longest to shortest wavelength the answer is:

wavelength is $5$ squares long
![The wave extends 1 square from the equilibrium level. There are 5 squares from a crest to the next crest.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/dbfc245f09bbca367f87ece9f5f70f86.svg)

wavelength is $4$ squares long
![The wave extends 3 squares from the equilibrium level. There are 4 squares from a crest to the next crest.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b0a0c26382883437578c64198dfe712a.svg)

wavelength is $3$ squares long
![The wave extends 2 squares from the equilibrium level. There are 3 squares from a crest to the next crest.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fae63c57e1119ad4289badbe669a4a54.svg)

wavelength is $2$ squares long
![The wave extends 4 squares from the equilibrium level. There are 2 squares from a crest to the next crest.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7523be33a445d0fefae3ded16fa7d797.svg)

correct: ![The wave extends 1 square from the equilibrium level. There are 5 squares from a crest to the next crest.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/dbfc245f09bbca367f87ece9f5f70f86.svg)
![The wave extends 3 squares from the equilibrium level. There are 4 squares from a crest to the next crest.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b0a0c26382883437578c64198dfe712a.svg)
![The wave extends 2 squares from the equilibrium level. There are 3 squares from a crest to the next crest.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fae63c57e1119ad4289badbe669a4a54.svg)
![The wave extends 4 squares from the equilibrium level. There are 2 squares from a crest to the next crest.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7523be33a445d0fefae3ded16fa7d797.svg)

assessment_items.question_content:
Snapshots of four periodic waves are modeled below. Each wave is modeled on the same grid.

**Rank the waves from shortest wavelength to longest wavelength.**

*shortest wavelength*

[[☃ sorter 1]]

*longest wavelength*
hints: **Wavelength** is the distance from the beginning of one wave cycle to the beginning of the next cycle.

Wavelength can be measured from a crest to the next crest, or from a trough to the next trough. So, to compare the wavelengths we can count the number of squares from a peak to the next peak, or from a trough to the next trough.

*Note that wavelength is separate from amplitude. On the models above, amplitude can be measured as how far the wave extends above and below the equilibrium line.*

From shortest to longest wavelength the answer is:

wavelength is $2$ squares long
![The wave extends 4 squares from the equilibrium level. There are 2 squares from a crest to the next crest.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7523be33a445d0fefae3ded16fa7d797.svg)

wavelength is $3$ squares long
![The wave extends 1 square from the equilibrium level. There are 3 squares from a crest to the next crest.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a59c0c65f0a68f8e6f0e59cde805a725.svg)

wavelength is $4$ squares long
![The wave extends 2 squares from the equilibrium level. There are 4 squares from a crest to the next crest.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4c72d96f00c8ad645b493a74492273e4.svg)

wavelength is $5$ squares long
![The wave extends 3 squares from the equilibrium level. There are 5 squares from a crest to the next crest.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0e672c7940e2c94df981407748747153.svg)

correct: ![The wave extends 4 squares from the equilibrium level. There are 2 squares from a crest to the next crest.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7523be33a445d0fefae3ded16fa7d797.svg)
![The wave extends 1 square from the equilibrium level. There are 3 squares from a crest to the next crest.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a59c0c65f0a68f8e6f0e59cde805a725.svg)
![The wave extends 2 squares from the equilibrium level. There are 4 squares from a crest to the next crest.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4c72d96f00c8ad645b493a74492273e4.svg)
![The wave extends 3 squares from the equilibrium level. There are 5 squares from a crest to the next crest.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0e672c7940e2c94df981407748747153.svg)

assessment_items.question_content:
A snapshot of a periodic wave is modeled below. Each square on the grid represents $1\,\text{unit}.$

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the wave's amplitude?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{units}$
hints: As a wave travels through a medium, it causes the medium to be temporarily changed from its [[☃ definition 1]]. The maximum amount that the medium is changed from equilibrium is the wave's **amplitude**.
On the model, the bold horizontal line represents the equilibrium level. The wave's amplitude can be measured from the equilibrium level to a crest, or from the equilibrium level to a trough.

Counting the squares from the equilibrium line up to a crest gives $2\,\text{units}.$

Counting the squares from the equilibrium line down to a trough also gives $2\,\text{units}.$
The wave's amplitude is $2\,\text{units}.$

options.alt: The wave extends 2 squares from the equilibrium level. There are 4 squares from a crest to the next crest.

assessment_items.question_content:
A boy notices periodic waves moving across the surface of a lake. A snapshot of the waves at one instant is shown below.

 [[☃ image 3]]

The boy wants to measure the waves' properties. He has a meterstick, a stopwatch, and a camera he can use to capture video.

**How could he use these materials to measure the amplitude of the waves?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Photo of repeating waves on the surface of a lake.

choices.content: Hold the meterstick vertically in the water. Capture video of the waves from the side. Scroll through the video to find the highest and lowest levels reached by water on the meterstick as waves pass. Find the difference of these values and divide it by two.

Hold the meterstick horizontally near the water's surface, pointing in the direction the waves are moving. Capture video of the waves from above. Pause the video to find the distance between one crest and the next crest on the meterstick.

Capture video of the waves from above while holding the camera still. Play the video and count how many wave crests enter one side of the screen during the video. Divide this number by the total time of the video.

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
A woman is exercising with heavy ropes. She moves her arms up and down in a repeating pattern, sending waves down the ropes as shown below.

 [[☃ image 2]]

The instructions for the first two parts of her workout are:

* **Part 1:** Move arms up and down once every second.

* **Part 2:** Move arms up and down twice every second. Move arms the same vertical distance as in Part 1.

**Compare the periodic waves she makes in each part of the workout.**

From part 1 to part 2 of the workout, the frequency [[☃ dropdown 1]], the wavelength [[☃ dropdown 2]], and the amplitude [[☃ dropdown 3]]. The energy transferred down the rope every second [[☃ dropdown 4]].
hints: From part 1 to part 2 of the workout, the woman moves her arms up and down *more frequently*. So, more frequent wave cycles will travel down the rope. In part 1, one wave cycle travels down the rope every second. In part 2, two wave cycles travel down the rope every second. The frequency increases.

Though the frequency increases, *the wave speed doesn't change* because she uses the *same ropes* in parts 1 and 2. So, individual wave cycles travel down the rope at the same speed, regardless of how frequently they're created. This means the more frequent waves in part 2 must be *closer together* along the rope. The wavelength decreases.

Part 2 of the workout says that she should move her arms vertically the same distance as in part 1. This distance corresponds to the amplitude of the waves created (how high the rope moves up). Since this doesn't change from part 1 to part 2, the amplitude stays the same.

Each wave cycle transfers energy down the rope. The amplitude of the waves doesn't change, so each cycle contains the same energy in parts 1 and 2. However, the cycles are more frequent in part 2, so more energy is transferred down the rope every second.

The answer is:

From part 1 to part 2 of the workout, the frequency increases, the wavelength decreases, and the amplitude stays the same. The energy transferred down the rope every second increases.

options.alt: Photo of a woman exercising with two thick ropes. She holds the end of a rope in each hand and moves her arms up and down, causing waves to travel down the rope away from her.

assessment_items.question_content:
A woman is exercising with heavy ropes. She moves her arms up and down in a repeating pattern, sending waves down the ropes as shown below.

 [[☃ image 2]]

The instructions for the first two parts of her workout are:

* **Part 1:** Move arms up and down twice every second.

* **Part 2:** Move arms up and down once every second. Move arms the same vertical distance as in Part 1.

**Compare the periodic waves she makes in each part of the workout.**

From part 1 to part 2 of the workout, the frequency [[☃ dropdown 1]], the wavelength [[☃ dropdown 2]], and the amplitude [[☃ dropdown 3]]. The energy transferred down the rope every second [[☃ dropdown 4]].
hints: From part 1 to part 2 of the workout, the woman moves her arms up and down *less frequently*. So, less frequent wave cycles will travel down the rope. In part 1, two wave cycles travel down the rope every second. In part 2, one wave cycle travels down the rope every second. The frequency decreases.

Though the frequency decreases, *the wave speed doesn't change* because she uses the *same ropes* in parts 1 and 2. Individual wave cycles travel down the rope at the same speed, regardless of how frequently they're created. This means the less frequent waves in part 2 must be *farther apart* along the rope. The wavelength increases.

Part 2 of the workout says that she should move her arms vertically the same distance as in part 1. This distance corresponds to the amplitude of the waves created (how high the rope moves up). Since this doesn't change from part 1 to part 2, the amplitude stays the same.

Each wave cycle transfers energy down the rope. The amplitude of the waves doesn't change, so each cycle contains the same energy in parts 1 and 2. However, the cycles are less frequent in part 2, so less energy is transferred down the rope every second.

The answer is:

From part 1 to part 2 of the workout, the frequency decreases, the wavelength increases, and the amplitude stays the same. The energy transferred down the rope every second decreases.

options.alt: Photo of a woman exercising with two thick ropes. She holds the end of a rope in each hand and moves her arms up and down, causing waves to travel down the rope away from her.

assessment_items.question_content:
A woman is exercising with heavy ropes. She moves her arms up and down in a repeating pattern, sending waves down the ropes as shown below.

 [[☃ image 2]]

The instructions for the first two parts of her workout are:

* **Part 1:** Move arms up and down once every second. Move arms vertically from shoulder to waist level.

* **Part 2:** Move arms up and down once every second. Move arms vertically from head to knee level.

**Compare the periodic waves she makes in each part of the workout.**

From part 1 to part 2 of the workout, the frequency [[☃ dropdown 1]], the wavelength [[☃ dropdown 2]], and the amplitude [[☃ dropdown 3]]. The energy transferred down the rope every second [[☃ dropdown 4]].
hints: From part 1 to part 2 of the workout, the woman moves her arms up and down with equal frequency. In part 1, one wave cycle travels down the rope every second. In part 2, one wave cycle travels down the rope every second. The frequency stays the same.

The *wave speed doesn't change* because she uses the *same ropes* in parts 1 and 2.  Individual wave cycles travel down the rope at the same speed in parts 1 and 2. Since the frequency is also the same in parts 1 and 2, this means the waves must have the *same spacing* along the rope in both parts. The wavelength stays the same.

Part 1 says move her arms vertically from shoulder to waist level. Part 2 says move arms from head to knee level. Therefore, her arms move a *greater vertical distance* in part 2. This distance corresponds to the amplitude of the waves created (how high the rope moves up). The amplitude increases.

Each wave cycle transfers energy down the rope. The frequency of the cycles doesn't change from part 1 to part 2. However, the amplitude is greater in part 2. So each cycle contains more energy in part 2, and more energy is transferred down the rope every second.

The answer is:

From part 1 to part 2 of the workout, the frequency stays the same, the wavelength stays the same, and the amplitude increases. The energy transferred down the rope every second increases.

options.alt: Photo of a woman exercising with two thick ropes. She holds the end of a rope in each hand and moves her arms up and down, causing waves to travel down the rope away from her.
